schools.summary: Victorinus: the four angels and the angel ascending with the seal of the living God point to the restraining of judgment until the servants of God are sealed and established against the great and intolerable persecution; John seeing four angels at the four corners of the earth holding the four winds, that no wind should blow on earth, sea, or tree — the restraining of judgment until God's servants are sealed (between the sixth and seventh seals, John seeing a pause and a sealing: 'four angels standing on the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds of the earth, that the wind should not blow on the earth, nor on the sea, nor on any tree'; the figure, that the 'four winds,' held back by the angels, are the destroying judgments of God, war, calamity, desolation, ready to be loosed upon the world, the four angels 'holding' them signifying that these judgments are under God's restraint, kept back for a time so that no harm comes to 'earth,' 'sea,' or 'tree' until a needful work is done, so God, in his providence, holds back the storm of judgment until his own people are made safe).

themes: four angels standing on the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds
that the wind should not blow on the earth, nor on the sea, nor on any tree
the destroying judgments held back under God's restraint
the storm stayed until God's people are sealed

schools.summary: Victorinus: the angel ascending from the east with the seal of the living God is the herald of restoration, sealing and establishing the churches before the loosing of judgment; John seeing another angel ascend from the east with the seal of the living God, crying to the four angels who were to hurt the earth and sea — the angel with God's seal staying the judgment to mark out God's own (John seeing a further messenger: 'another angel ascending from the east, having the seal of the living God'; the two marks of this angel and his errand, that he comes 'from the east,' the quarter of light and of God's blessing, bearing 'the seal of the living God,' the instrument by which God's people are to be marked as his own, and 'he cried with a loud voice to the four angels, to whom it was given to hurt the earth and the sea,' charging them to stay their destroying work; the gracious order of God's providence, that before the judgments are loosed the sealing angel goes forth to mark out and secure God's people, that they may be known and kept, so God ever cares for his own before he sends the storm).

themes: another angel ascending from the east, having the seal of the living God
he cried with a loud voice to the four angels
the sealing angel staying the judgment
God marking out his own before the storm

schools.summary: Gill: in the historicist reading, the commission of the four angels to hurt was not yet to be carried out, nor taken away by Christ, but at the appointed time, at the blowing of the several trumpets, they let loose the winds and let in the Goths, Huns, and Vandals; the sealing of God's servants comes first; John hearing the angel say, 'Hurt not the earth, the sea, or the trees, till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads' — the staying of all harm until God's servants are marked on the forehead (the sealing angel's word recorded: 'Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, nor the trees, till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads'; the purpose and manner of the sealing, that the judgments are held back until 'the servants of our God' are 'sealed,' marked out as his own and secured against the coming harm, and that 'in their foreheads,' an open, visible mark of ownership, as the Spirit seals believers and sets God's mark upon them; the comfort, that God will not let the storm fall until every one of his own is sealed and safe, so the believer is divinely marked and kept, distinguished and preserved amid the judgments that fall on the world).

themes: Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, nor the trees
till we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads
the staying of all harm until God's servants are sealed
the believer marked and kept amid judgment

schools.summary: Gill: John heard the number of them which were sealed, and therefore could be sure of the exact number, which did not depend upon his sight and telling them (in which some mistake might be made) but he heard the number expressed; John hearing that the number sealed was a hundred and forty-four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel — the definite, complete number of the sealed drawn from Israel's tribes (John, having seen the sealing, now 'heard the number of them which were sealed: and there were sealed an hundred and forty and four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel'; the significance of the number, that 144,000, twelve thousand from each of twelve tribes, is a definite, ordered, and complete number, marking that God knows and secures the exact tale of his own, not one missing; the debate on 'the tribes of the children of Israel,' some taking it of literal, ethnic Israel, a remnant of the Jews sealed and preserved, others of the spiritual Israel, the whole church of God under the figure of the twelve tribes, the symbolic number standing for the full and perfect number of the elect, either way the sealed being a complete and known company, kept by God).

debates.question: Who are the 144,000 sealed 'of all the tribes of the children of Israel'?

sides.position: Literal, ethnic Israel — a remnant of the Jews sealed and preserved
The spiritual Israel — the whole church of God under the figure of the twelve tribes, the symbolic number standing for the full number of the elect

themes: and I heard the number of them which were sealed
an hundred and forty and four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel
the definite, complete number of the sealed
God knowing the exact tale of his own, none missing

schools.summary: Gill: Judah is mentioned first, because Christ sprang from that tribe, and the pure worship of God was preserved in it, and it was preserved a distinct tribe until the coming of Shiloh; its name signifies 'praise God' and shows the sealed are gathered to praise; John recording twelve thousand sealed of Judah, of Reuben, and of Gad — the equal sealing of each tribe, Judah first for Christ's sake (John beginning the roll of the sealed tribe by tribe: 'of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand,' and so of Reuben and Gad; two things, first the equal number, 'twelve thousand' from each tribe, marking that none is favoured or neglected, each having its full and equal share among the sealed, and second the order, that 'Juda' is named first not by natural birthright, for Reuben was the firstborn, but because of Christ who sprang out of Juda and because the worship of God was preserved in it; Judah's name signifying 'praise,' a fitting head for the company of the sealed who are gathered to praise God, so the sealing is orderly, equal, and headed by the tribe of the Messiah).

themes: of the tribe of Juda were sealed twelve thousand
of the tribe of Reuben... of the tribe of Gad were sealed twelve thousand
the equal sealing of each tribe
Judah named first for Christ who sprang from it

schools.summary: Gill: Asher, which the Septuagint pronounce 'Aser' as here, signifies 'blessed,' as all the sealed ones are, blessed with all spiritual blessings, with grace here and glory hereafter; John recording twelve thousand sealed of Asher, of Naphtali, and of Manasses — the roll of the sealed continued, each tribe's name bearing a fitting sense (John continuing the equal sealing through the next tribes: 'of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand,' and so of Naphtali and Manasses; the commentators observing a fitness in the very names, 'Asher' signifying 'blessed,' fitting for the sealed who are blessed with all spiritual blessings, grace now and glory to come; the steady, equal continuance of the count, twelve thousand from each, the full number gathered from every tribe, so the sealing proceeds without favour or omission, each tribe yielding its appointed company of the redeemed, the names themselves whispering the blessedness of those whom God has sealed).

themes: of the tribe of Aser were sealed twelve thousand
of the tribe of Nepthalim... of the tribe of Manasses were sealed twelve thousand
the roll of the sealed continued, each tribe equal
the names fitting, 'Asher' meaning 'blessed'

schools.summary: Gill: the name of the tribe of Simeon signifies 'hearing,' and such a hearing as is attended with obedience, and denotes the spiritual and practical hearing of the gospel by those who hear, and understand, and believe, and practise what they hear; John recording twelve thousand sealed of Simeon, of Levi, and of Issachar — the roll continued, the names again whispering the graces of the sealed (John continuing: 'of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand,' and so of Levi and Issachar; the fitness the commentators find in the names, 'Simeon' signifying 'hearing,' the spiritual obedient hearing of the gospel that marks the truly sealed who hear, understand, believe, and obey, and even Levi, the priestly tribe, having its twelve thousand, none excluded from the sealed company; the steady equal count going on, each tribe yielding its full number, so the sealing of God's people is complete, orderly, and gathered from every quarter, the very names of the tribes adorned with the graces of those whom God seals).

themes: of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand
of the tribe of Levi... of the tribe of Issachar were sealed twelve thousand
the roll continued, each tribe yielding its full number
'Simeon' meaning hearing, the obedient hearing of the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: Zebulun signifies 'dwelling,' and was the tribe in which Christ had his dwelling and where he much conversed, and may denote his gracious inhabitation, as well as that of God the Father and of the Spirit, among the saints and sealed; John recording twelve thousand sealed of Zebulun, of Joseph, and of Benjamin, completing the number — the roll of the sealed brought to its full and complete sum (John closing the list: 'of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed twelve thousand,' and so of Joseph and Benjamin, completing the twelve tribes and the full 144,000; the fitness in the names, 'Zebulun' signifying 'dwelling,' the tribe where Christ much dwelt, fitting for the sealed in whom Christ and the Father and the Spirit graciously dwell; the completion of the sealing, that every tribe has now yielded its twelve thousand, the whole company of the sealed gathered and sure, not one tribe omitted, not one of the appointed number lacking, so the sealing of God's people is full, complete, and final, every one of his own marked and kept before the judgments fall).

themes: of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed twelve thousand
of the tribe of Joseph... of the tribe of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand
the roll closed, completing the 144,000
the sealing full and final, every one of God's own marked

schools.summary: Gill: this is a distinct vision from the preceding one and not a continuation of it (as if the sealing of the Jewish believers was meant in the former and the sealing of Gentiles in this); here is no mention made of sealing or numbering, for this great multitude is gathered out of all nations, an innumerable company; John beholding a great multitude no man could number, of all nations, before the throne and the Lamb, in white robes with palms — the countless redeemed of every nation standing in triumph before the throne (John, after the sealing of the numbered tribes, seeing a wider and more glorious vision: 'a great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations, and kindreds, and people, and tongues, stood before the throne, and before the Lamb'; the breadth and glory of this company, innumerable, beyond all counting, drawn 'of all nations,' not Israel only but the redeemed of every people, kindred, and tongue, standing 'before the throne, and before the Lamb,' in God's immediate presence, 'clothed with white robes,' the robes of purity, victory, and the righteousness of Christ, 'and palms in their hands,' the palms of victory and festal joy, so the sealed are seen at last triumphant and glorified, a vast multitude of every nation gathered safe before God and the Lamb).

themes: a great multitude, which no man could number, of all nations
stood before the throne, and before the Lamb
clothed with white robes, and palms in their hands
the countless redeemed of every nation, triumphant

schools.summary: Gill: they cried with a loud voice, to show the strength of their affection, the greatness of their joy, and how sensible they were of the favour they enjoyed, and how hearty they were in the ascription of glory to God and the Lamb; John hearing the multitude cry with a loud voice, 'Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb' — the redeemed ascribing their whole salvation to God and the Lamb (the great multitude lifting a triumphant song: 'Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne, and unto the Lamb'; the substance of their praise, that they ascribe 'salvation' wholly to God and to the Lamb, owning that their deliverance and glory are not their own work but the gift and achievement of 'our God,' the Father on the throne, and 'the Lamb,' Christ who redeemed them by his blood; the loudness and strength of the cry, the measure of their joy and gratitude, so the redeemed in glory give all the praise of their salvation to the Father and the Son, claiming nothing for themselves but crowning God and the Lamb with the glory of saving them).

themes: and cried with a loud voice, Salvation to our God which sitteth upon the throne
and unto the Lamb
the redeemed ascribing salvation wholly to God and the Lamb
claiming nothing for themselves, crowning God and the Lamb

schools.summary: Gill: all the angels, the holy and elect angels, even all of them, the ten thousand times ten thousand and thousands of thousands, the innumerable company, who are represented in the same position, stood round about the throne and about the elders and the four living creatures; John seeing all the angels stand about the throne, the elders, and the four beasts, and fall on their faces and worship God — the angelic host joining the redeemed in worship before the throne (John seeing the heavenly worship widen to include the angels: 'all the angels stood round about the throne, and about the elders and the four beasts, and fell before the throne on their faces, and worshipped God'; the order and reverence, that the innumerable host of angels, encircling the throne, the elders representing the redeemed church, and the four living creatures, join the worship, 'falling on their faces' in the deepest reverence and adoration, the angels, though they have no part in the salvation the redeemed celebrate, yet rejoicing in it and worshipping the God who saves, so heaven's whole company, redeemed and angels alike, bows before the throne in united adoration of God).

themes: and all the angels stood round about the throne, and about the elders and the four beasts
fell before the throne on their faces, and worshipped God
the angelic host joining the redeemed in worship
the angels rejoicing in a salvation they share not

schools.summary: Gill: the angels say 'Amen,' approving and confirming what the great multitude of men had said in ascribing the glory of salvation to God and the Lamb; the angels, though they have no part in it themselves, yet highly approve of it as right and just that men should give the glory of it where it is due; John hearing the angels say, 'Amen: Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might, be unto our God for ever and ever. Amen' — the angels' full and hearty doxology, sealing the praise of the redeemed (the angels answering the redeemed multitude's song with their own great doxology, framed within a double 'Amen': they first confirm the multitude's praise, 'Amen,' then heap up a sevenfold ascription, 'Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might,' all to be 'unto our God for ever and ever,' and seal it with a final 'Amen'; the completeness of the praise, seven attributes, the number of perfection, and the heartiness of the angels who, though not themselves redeemed, gladly own that all blessing and glory belong to God for ever, so the worship of heaven swells in fullest praise to the saving God).

themes: Amen: Blessing, and glory, and wisdom, and thanksgiving, and honour, and power, and might
be unto our God for ever and ever. Amen
the angels' sevenfold doxology to God
the fullest praise sealing the redeemed's song

schools.summary: Gill: this elder was not the Apostle Peter (as some Popish interpreters have thought), still less Pope Sylvester who lived in Constantine's time, but one of the four and twenty elders who puts the question to draw out and instruct John; John recording that one of the elders asked him who the white-robed company were and whence they came — the elder's question opening the way to reveal who the redeemed multitude are (one of the elders, representing the redeemed church, turning to John with a question: 'What are these which are arrayed in white robes? and whence came they?'; the purpose of the question, that it is not asked from the elder's ignorance but, in the manner of teachers, to draw out John's attention and to lead to the gracious answer that follows, the question fixing the eye on the white-robed multitude and prompting the seer to consider who they are and how they came to such glory, so the elder by his question opens the way for the revealing of the identity and history of the redeemed throng, the answer being given in the verses that follow).

themes: and one of the elders answered, saying unto me
What are these which are arrayed in white robes? and whence came they?
the elder's question about the redeemed multitude
asked to draw out John and open the gracious answer

schools.summary: Gill: John replies in a very humble, modest, and respectful manner to the elder, calling him 'Sir' according to the usage of the eastern people, and owning that the elder knew rather than himself; John saying 'Sir, thou knowest,' and the elder answering that these came out of great tribulation and washed their robes white in the blood of the Lamb — the redeemed identified as those who came through tribulation and were cleansed by the Lamb's blood (John, with humble reverence, owning 'Sir, thou knowest,' deferring to the elder, and the elder giving the great answer: 'These are they which came out of great tribulation, and have washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb'; the twofold description, first their history of suffering, 'came out of great tribulation,' passed through severe trials and persecutions on earth, and second the ground of their purity and glory, 'washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb'; the paradox and the truth, that their robes are made 'white' by being washed 'in the blood' of Christ, for it is his atoning blood alone that cleanses from sin and clothes the soul in spotless righteousness, so the redeemed owe their whiteness not to their own merit or their sufferings but wholly to the cleansing blood of the Lamb).

debates.question: To what do the redeemed owe their white robes?

sides.position: Wholly to the atoning blood of the Lamb — their robes made white by being washed in his blood, not by their own merit or even their sufferings; his blood alone cleanses from sin

themes: these are they which came out of great tribulation
have washed their robes, and made them white in the blood of the Lamb
the redeemed cleansed by the Lamb's blood
their whiteness owed not to merit but to Christ's blood

schools.summary: Gill: they are before the throne not because of their great tribulations (as if those merited it) nor because of their robes or conversation garments, but because they were brought through them and out of them by the grace and power of God, and because those robes were washed and made white in the blood of the Lamb; the elder saying that therefore they are before God's throne, serving him day and night in his temple, and he that sits on the throne shall dwell among them — the redeemed honoured to serve God unceasingly and to have God dwell with them (the elder describing the blessedness of the cleansed: 'therefore are they before the throne of God,' admitted to God's immediate presence on the ground of the Lamb's blood not their own works, 'and serve him day and night in his temple,' their everlasting employment being the joyful, unwearied service and worship of God, 'day and night,' continually, without ceasing or weariness, 'and he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them'; the crowning blessing, that God himself 'shall dwell among them,' spreading his tabernacle over them, his immediate abiding presence their eternal joy and shelter, so the redeemed, cleansed by the blood, are brought to unending service of God and the everlasting dwelling of God with them).

themes: therefore are they before the throne of God, and serve him day and night in his temple
he that sitteth on the throne shall dwell among them
the redeemed serving God unceasingly
God himself dwelling among them, their eternal joy

schools.summary: Gill: the words are taken out of Isaiah (49:10) and will be true in a literal and corporeal sense — the saints are now often in hunger and thirst, then they shall be so no more — and in a mystical and spiritual sense, there will be no famine of the word; the elder declaring that they shall hunger no more, nor thirst, nor shall the sun or any heat smite them — the end of all want, hunger, thirst, and scorching for the glorified (the elder setting out the negative blessedness of heaven, the removal of every want and pain: 'they shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more; neither shall the sun light on them, nor any heat'; the comfort, that all the wants and distresses the saints knew on earth are forever ended, 'hunger' and 'thirst,' every craving and lack of body and soul, shall be no more, and 'the sun' and 'heat,' the scorching trials, persecutions, and burning sorrows of this life, shall never again beat upon them; the borrowing from Isaiah's prophecy of the comfort of God's people, so the glorified are wholly delivered from every form of want, pain, and affliction, in a state where no lack or burning trouble can ever reach them).

themes: they shall hunger no more, neither thirst any more
neither shall the sun light on them, nor any heat
the end of all want and distress for the glorified
every craving and scorching trial forever ended

schools.summary: Gill: the Lamb is in the midst of the throne, not before it as the great multitude are said to be, nor round about it as the angels, but in the midst, being equal to him that sits upon it, sitting on the same throne with him and having the same power; the elder declaring that the Lamb in the midst of the throne shall feed them and lead them to living fountains, and God shall wipe away all tears — the Lamb the Shepherd of the glorified, and God the comforter who ends all weeping (the elder closing with the tenderest picture of heaven's blessedness: 'the Lamb which is in the midst of the throne shall feed them, and shall lead them unto living fountains of waters: and God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes'; the sweet reversal, that the Lamb, who was slain, is now the Shepherd of his flock, 'in the midst of the throne,' on the throne equal with God, who shall 'feed them' as a shepherd his sheep and 'lead them unto living fountains of waters,' to the ever-flowing springs of life and joy, the fulness of God himself, and the crowning comfort, 'God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes,' every sorrow ended, every grief comforted by God's own tender hand, so the redeemed are forever fed, led, and comforted, all their tears wiped away by God, in unending blessedness).

themes: for the Lamb which is in the midst of the throne shall feed them
shall lead them unto living fountains of waters
God shall wipe away all tears from their eyes
the slain Lamb now the Shepherd, every grief comforted
